entries.title: Dog Walking Frequency
facts: Adult dogs generally need at least 30 minutes to 2 hours of exercise per day depending on breed and age.
High-energy breeds (Labrador, Border Collie, Husky) require 60-90 minutes of vigorous daily exercise.
Low-energy or senior dogs may be satisfied with two 15-minute walks per day.
Puppies under 6 months should have exercise limited to 5 minutes per month of age, twice daily, to protect developing joints.
Walking in the morning is recommended to help dogs eliminate overnight waste and set a calm tone for the day.
Skipping walks for multiple consecutive days can increase anxiety and destructive behavior in dogs.

entries.title: Dog Feeding Guidelines
facts: Adult dogs should typically be fed twice daily — once in the morning and once in the evening.
Puppies under 6 months generally need three to four meals per day.
Senior dogs (7+ years) may benefit from smaller, more frequent meals to ease digestion.
Always provide fresh water alongside meals.
Avoid feeding large dogs immediately before or after vigorous exercise to reduce bloat risk.
Consistent feeding times help regulate digestion and set behavioral expectations.

entries.title: Administering Medication to Dogs
facts: Most oral dog medications can be hidden in a small amount of food like peanut butter or a pill pocket.
Never skip a scheduled medication dose without consulting a veterinarian.
Administer medications at the same time each day to maintain consistent blood levels.
Topical flea/tick treatments should be applied to the base of the neck where the dog cannot lick.
Log each dose given; double-dosing is dangerous and easy to accidentally do with multiple caretakers.
Store all medications as directed — some require refrigeration.

entries.title: Dog Grooming Basics
facts: Short-haired dogs generally need brushing once a week; long-haired breeds may need daily brushing.
Most dogs need bathing every 4 to 6 weeks, though active or outdoor dogs may need more frequent baths.
Check and clean ears weekly to prevent infection, especially in floppy-eared breeds.
Trim nails every 3 to 4 weeks; overgrown nails can cause pain and posture problems.
Brush teeth daily or at least 3 times per week to prevent periodontal disease.
Start grooming routines early in a dog's life to build tolerance and comfort.

entries.title: Dog Mental Enrichment
facts: Dogs need mental stimulation in addition to physical exercise to prevent boredom and anxiety.
Puzzle feeders and snuffle mats give dogs an outlet for natural foraging instincts.
Training sessions of 5 to 15 minutes count as enrichment and strengthen the human-dog bond.
Rotating toys every few days keeps novelty high and maintains engagement.
Social play with other dogs is enriching for most social breeds.
Lack of enrichment is a common cause of destructive behavior like chewing and excessive barking.

entries.title: Cat Feeding Guidelines
facts: Adult cats can be fed twice daily or via controlled free-feeding depending on the cat's weight goals.
Wet food is recommended at least partially because cats have low natural thirst drive and benefit from moisture in food.
Kittens under 6 months need three to four small meals per day.
Senior cats (10+ years) may need specialized senior-formula food with adjusted protein and phosphorus levels.
Do not leave uneaten wet food out for more than 30 minutes to prevent bacterial growth.
Avoid sudden food changes — transition over 7 to 10 days to prevent digestive upset.

entries.title: Administering Medication to Cats
facts: Cats are notoriously difficult to medicate; a pill gun or pill pocket can make the process easier.
Liquid medications can sometimes be mixed with a small amount of palatable food.
Never give a cat medication intended for dogs or humans without veterinary guidance.
After giving an oral pill, offer a small amount of water or wet food to ensure it travels to the stomach.
Scruffing (gently holding the back of the neck) can temporarily calm a resistant cat during medication.
Topical medications applied between the shoulder blades are harder for cats to reach and lick off.

entries.title: Cat Grooming Basics
facts: Short-haired cats usually self-groom adequately but benefit from weekly brushing to reduce hairballs.
Long-haired cats (Persians, Maine Coons) need daily brushing to prevent painful mats.
Cats rarely need baths unless they get into something or have a skin condition.
Trim cat nails every 2 to 3 weeks to prevent snagging and over-curling into paw pads.
Dental disease affects over 70% of cats by age 3 — weekly teeth brushing is recommended.
Check ears weekly for wax buildup, odor, or discharge, which may indicate infection or mites.

entries.title: Cat Mental Enrichment
facts: Indoor cats need at least 15 to 30 minutes of active play per day to meet their predatory instincts.
Wand toys and laser pointers simulate prey movement and provide satisfying exercise.
Window perches with an outdoor view provide passive enrichment through visual and olfactory stimulation.
Puzzle feeders slow eating and engage problem-solving instincts in cats.
Cats generally prefer several short play sessions over one long one.
Lack of enrichment in indoor cats correlates with obesity, over-grooming, and aggression.

entries.title: General Medication Safety
facts: Always complete the full course of antibiotics even if symptoms disappear early.
Never share medications between pets of different species without explicit veterinary approval.
Store medications away from food, heat, and humidity.
Keep a medication log with the date, time, and dose to share with your vet at checkups.
If a dose is missed, do not double-dose on the next scheduled time unless your vet instructs otherwise.
Be aware of side effects and contact a vet immediately if unusual symptoms appear after medicating.

entries.title: Water and Hydration for Pets
facts: Fresh water should be available to all pets at all times.
Wash water bowls daily to prevent biofilm and bacterial buildup.
Some pets prefer running water — pet fountains can encourage cats and dogs to drink more.
Dehydration signs include lethargy, sunken eyes, dry gums, and reduced skin elasticity.
During hot weather or after vigorous exercise, offer water before food.

entries.title: Care Tips for Small Pets and Exotic Animals
facts: Rabbits, guinea pigs, birds, and reptiles each have species-specific care needs — consult a species-knowledgeable vet.
Small mammals like rabbits need at least 3 to 4 hours of supervised free-roam time daily.
Birds require mental enrichment through foraging toys, varied food presentation, and interaction.
Reptiles are ectothermic and depend on precisely maintained temperature gradients for health.
Routine veterinary checkups are critical for exotic pets since illness signs can be subtle until advanced.
Enrichment, social time, and mental stimulation are important for virtually every pet species.

entries.title: Morning Scheduling Best Practices
facts: Feeding and medication tasks are most reliably completed in the morning when daily routines are fresh.
Morning walks help dogs eliminate overnight waste and benefit from cooler temperatures.
Cats tend to be most active at dawn — morning play sessions align with their natural activity peaks.
High-priority tasks should generally be placed in the morning to ensure they are not missed.
Morning grooming can double as a health check — inspect eyes, ears, and coat condition.

entries.title: Managing a Tight Time Budget
facts: Combine grooming with health inspection to save time — brushing while checking ears and eyes takes no extra time.
Feeding takes 5 to 10 minutes of active time even for multiple pets if bowls are pre-set the night before.
Short enrichment sessions of 5 to 10 minutes are still meaningful and better than none at all.
Medication that is hidden in food requires minimal additional time if meals are already scheduled.
Group tasks that occur in the same location to reduce transition time.
Weekly grooming tasks can be broken into daily micro-sessions to spread the time cost.
